asin-tibuok.description: Aged for three months in clay pots over a wood-fed fire by the Bersales family in Alburquerque, Bohol. Each block looks like a dinosaur egg. Shave it over rice.

tultul-dokdok.description: Block sea salt boiled down for a full day from Guimaras seawater, then pressed into bricks. Shave it over kinilaw, hot rice, or sinugba.

asin-sa-bubu.description: Sea salt steamed and dried inside bamboo pipes in Pasuquin, Ilocos Norte. The bamboo flavor stays on the salt. Sold from roadside stalls along the Maharlika Highway.

guimaras-mangoes.description: The country's only mango variety with geographical indication status. Honey-sweet, peak season April to June. Manggahan Festival is the third week of May in Jordan.

camiguin-lanzones.description: Volcanic-soil lanzones, smaller and sweeter than the standard Laguna kind. Lanzones Festival is the third week of October in Mambajao. Bring as pasalubong.

kapeng-barako.description: Liberica coffee beans from the Batangas-Cavite uplands, bold and earthy. The kind your lolo brewed in the morning. Cafe de Lipa still bags it for pasalubong.

sagada-arabica.description: Highland Arabica grown above 1,500 meters. Buy whole bean from Sagada Brew, Bana's Cafe, or the Saturday market and grind at home for the freshest cup.

sulu-civet-coffee.description: Tausug-brewed civet coffee with cardamom and ginger, called Kahawa Sug. Served thick in tiny cups. The Sulu State College runs a small-batch program.

bicol-pili-nuts.description: Volcanic-soil pili nuts from Albay and Sorsogon, buttery and crunchy. Sold honey-glazed, salted, or pressed into pulp. JRS Pili Sweets in Legazpi is the classic stop.

cordillera-heritage-rice.description: Ominio, Tinawon, and Unoy heirloom strains grown on the Banaue and Kalinga terraces. RICE Inc. and the Cordillera Heirloom Rice Project sell direct from the coops.

malagos-chocolate.description: Davao's bean-to-bar chocolate made at the Puentespina farm in Calinan. The 65% dark took bronze at the International Chocolate Awards in 2017.

sukang-paombong.description: Nipa palm sap vinegar naturally fermented in Paombong, Bulacan. The acid is sharper and rounder than supermarket cane vinegar. Sold along the Manila North Road.

sukang-iloko.description: Sugarcane vinegar fermented in burnay jars for two years. Also called sukang Jamon. The standard dip for bagnet, dinakdakan, and pinakbet.

davao-puyat-durian.description: The local Puyat variety, creamier and less pungent than the Thai durians on supermarket shelves. Magsaysay Park in Davao is the main vendor row.

tnalak-cloth.description: Sacred abaca cloth woven by T'boli dreamweavers in Lake Sebu. The patterns come in dreams from their patron spirit. Lang Dulay's lineage still runs the School of Living Traditions.

inabel-iloko.description: Cotton textile loom-woven in Pinili and Vigan. Geometric jacquards used for blankets, towels, and table runners. Magdalena Gamayo is the matriarch of the craft.

yakan-tennun.description: Colorful geometric weave from the Yakan tribe of Basilan. Sells direct at the Yakan Weaving Village along Upper Calarian Road in Zamboanga City.

pina-cloth.description: Ultra-fine luxury fabric handwoven from pineapple leaf fibers in Kalibo, Aklan. The base cloth for high-end barong Tagalog. About 30 fibers go into one strand.

hablon.description: Glossy handwoven silk and cotton from Miag-ao weavers in Iloilo. The Indag-an Multipurpose Cooperative runs the looms. Sold as bolts and finished panuelos.

dagmay.description: Mandaya tribe's mud-dyed abaca cloth from Davao Oriental. Earthy reds and browns. The Mandaya Weaving Center in Cateel sells direct from the loom.

mabal-tabih.description: Blaan tribe's ikat-dyed abaca weave from Sarangani. Made for tribal skirts, now sold as bolts, wall panels, and bags. The Blaan Tribal Heritage Studio runs the workshop.

pis-syabit.description: Tausug handwoven head cloth with geometric patterns. Originally a turban, now framed as wall art. Pis syabit weavers in Patikul still work it in cotton and silk.

basey-tikog-mats.description: Banig mats woven from tikog reed and hand-embroidered with bright florals. Sold rolled at the Basey Tikog Producers Association in Samar. Lasts a generation.

merida-banaca.description: Handwoven fabric blending banana and abaca fibers, sold by the Merida Banaca cooperative in Leyte. Used for bags, panels, and home decor.

burnay-jars.description: Unglazed earthenware jars spun on a foot-driven wheel in Pagburnayan, Vigan. Used to ferment bagoong, sukang Iloko, and basi wine. Fired in a brick dragon kiln.

paete-woodcarvings.description: Hand-carved santo figures, masks, and religious icons. Paete has been the country's carving capital since the 1500s. The Quezon Street main road is lined with shops.

pampanga-parol.description: Giant Kapampangan Christmas lanterns, wired and lit. The Giant Lantern Festival happens the Saturday before Christmas Eve in San Fernando.

marikina-leather-shoes.description: Handcrafted full-grain leather footwear from the Marikina shoe district. Custom orders from Ang Tibay, Risque, and Black Wing take about a week.

palawan-south-sea-pearls.description: Golden South Sea pearls, the country's signature gemstone. Jewelmer farms them in the Sulu Sea and sells at the Greenbelt 5 boutique in Makati.

capiz-shell-products.description: Translucent windowpane oyster shell lamps, chandeliers, and capiz parol. Roxas City wholesales worldwide. The Capiz Provincial Capitol sells the parol direct in December.

lumban-embroidery.description: Hand-embroidered piña and jusi cloth for traditional barong Tagalog. The Lumban embroiderers along Rizal Street still operate as a guild. Custom orders take three weeks.

tabaco-scissors.description: Hand-forged scissors, shears, and bolos from the Tabaco blade smiths along Tayhi Street. Heavier and sharper than commercial brands. Pasalubong with a serious edge.

balingasag-pottery.description: Traditional terracotta water jars, planters, and stoves from Balingasag, Misamis Oriental. Sold direct from the kiln near the Balingasag-Lagonglong road.

siquijor-lana.description: Herbal lana oil blended by traditional healers in San Antonio, Siquijor. Pasalubong for the curious. Locals rub it on muscle aches and joint pain.

bohol-calamay.description: Sticky sweet coconut-and-rice paste sealed inside two halved coconut shells. Jagna is the home town. Pry it open with a spoon over coffee.

silay-piaya.description: Flaky flatbread filled with muscovado sugar. Merci Bakeshop, Bong Bong's, and El Ideal in Silay and Bacolod still bake the original.

binagol-leyte.description: Sweet taro pudding cooked and served inside a halved coconut shell. Dagami, Leyte is the home town. Eat with a spoon over kapeng Barako.

moron-leyte.description: Twists of chocolate and milk-flavored sticky-rice cake, wrapped in banana leaf. Tacloban pasalubong. Sold along the Real Street and Imelda Avenue stalls.

camiguin-pastel.description: Soft buns filled with sweet custard or yema, originated by Vjandep in Mambajao, Camiguin. The original Vjandep store on J.P. Rizal Street still serves them warm.

tuguegarao-chicharabao.description: Crispy puffed carabao hide cracker from Tuguegarao, Cagayan. Drier than pork chicharon, lighter than rice crackers. Carbon Market sells them by the kilo bag.

carcar-chicharon.description: Pork rind crackers with backfat attached. Sold along the Carcar main road in Cebu by the kilo. Casa Real and the chicharon strip vendors stand out.

bacolod-barquillos.description: Thin, crunchy rolled wafer tubes. El Ideal Bakery on Lacson Street, Bacolod has been making them since 1920. The boxed pasalubong of Negros.

palapa-maranao.description: Maranao spicy condiment of sakurab scallion, ginger, chili, and grated coconut. Jarred for travel. Goes on grilled fish, rice, and noodles.

budbud-kabog.description: Rare kodo millet sticky cake wrapped in banana leaf. Catmon, Cebu is the home town. Eat with sikwate hot chocolate. Sold at the Catmon Public Market.

iligan-cheddar-halaya.description: Purple ube jam layered with cheddar cheese. The Iligan twist on Good Shepherd. Sold at Cheding's Peanuts, Roxas Pasalubong, and the Iligan terminal stalls.

good-shepherd-ube-jam.description: Made by the Good Shepherd nuns at the convent on Gibraltar Road in Baguio, sold in the iconic green-capped glass jar. Proceeds fund Cordillera scholarships.
